name: gpt

members.extended:
Args mirror the HuggingFace `OpenAIGPTConfig` field set so checkpoint
porting is a flat key rename.

members.name: GPTDoubleHeadsModel

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, mc_token_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None, mc_labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GPT-1 + LM head + multiple-choice classification head.
members.extended:
Implements the Radford 2018 §3.3 multi-task fine-tuning recipe.  Used
for tasks like RACE / Story Cloze where each example presents a prompt
followed by $C$ candidate completions; the model is jointly
trained with the standard LM objective on each choice and a
$C$-way classification loss picking the correct completion.

Inputs are 3-D: `input_ids` of shape `(N, C, L)` flattens to
`(N * C, L)` for the trunk and reshapes back to `(N, C, ...)` at
the heads.  `mc_token_ids` of shape `(N, C)` indexes the per-choice
pooling position (typically the trailing CLF token of each completion).

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Hyperparameters.  `config.tie_word_embeddings` (default True)
ties the LM head weight to the input embedding matrix.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import GPTConfig, GPTDoubleHeadsModel
>>> cfg = GPTConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=64,
...                 num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=256)
>>> model = GPTDoubleHeadsModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[[101, 7592, 102],
...                            [101, 2088, 102]]])    # (N=1, C=2, L=3)
>>> mc_ids = lucid.tensor([[2, 2]]).long()
>>> out = model(input_ids, mc_token_ids=mc_ids)
>>> out.mc_logits.shape   # (N=1, C=2)
(1, 2)
notes:
Reference: Radford, Narasimhan, Salimans, and Sutskever, *"Improving
Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical
Report, 2018, §3.3.

Combined loss when both objectives are supplied:



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{joint}}
    = \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{MC}}.
$$

attributes.name: lm_head
attributes.description: Tied output projection of shape `(hidden_size, vocab_size)`.

attributes.name: mc_head
attributes.description:
Per-choice pooled-token scalar projection of shape
`(hidden_size, 1)`.

members.name: GPTDoubleHeadsOutput
members.signature: GPTDoubleHeadsOutput(lm_logits: Tensor, mc_logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None, lm_loss: Tensor | None = None, mc_loss: Tensor | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(lm_logits: Tensor, mc_logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None, lm_loss: Tensor | None = None, mc_loss: Tensor | None = None)

members.extended:
Aggregates the per-choice causal-LM logits, per-choice classification
logits, and the optional per-objective and combined losses produced
when training Radford-style multiple-choice fine-tunes.

parameters.name: lm_logits
parameters.description:
LM head logits of shape `(N, C, L, vocab_size)` — one
distribution per token of every batch x choice pair.

parameters.name: mc_logits
parameters.description:
Multiple-choice head logits of shape `(N, C)` — one scalar per
candidate completion.

parameters.name: loss
parameters.description:
Sum of `lm_loss` and `mc_loss` when both are available;
otherwise the single available loss, or `None`.

parameters.name: lm_loss
parameters.description: Shift-cross-entropy on the LM head when `labels` is supplied.

parameters.name: mc_loss
parameters.description:
Cross-entropy on the multiple-choice head when `mc_labels` is
supplied.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import GPTConfig, GPTDoubleHeadsModel
>>> cfg = GPTConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=64,
...                 num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=256)
>>> model = GPTDoubleHeadsModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[[101, 7592, 102]]])      # (N=1, C=1, L=3)
>>> mc_ids = lucid.tensor([[2]]).long()
>>> out = model(input_ids, mc_token_ids=mc_ids)
>>> out.lm_logits.shape, out.mc_logits.shape
((1, 1, 3, 40478), (1, 1))
notes:
Reference: Radford, Narasimhan, Salimans, and Sutskever, *"Improving
Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical
Report, 2018, §3.3.

members.name: GPTForSequenceClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GPT-1 with a last-token sequence-classification head.
members.extended:
Wraps `GPTModel` with a dropout-regularised linear classifier
operating on the **rightmost non-pad token's** hidden state — the
canonical decoder-style pooling.  Unlike BERT (CLS at position 0),
decoder LMs only have a meaningful summary at the final attended
position because every preceding position has attended to a strict
prefix only.  When `attention_mask` is provided we honour it;
otherwise the last position of every row is used.

parameters.name: config

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import GPTConfig, GPTForSequenceClassification
>>> cfg = GPTConfig(num_labels=3, num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                 num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = GPTForSequenceClassification(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, num_labels=3)
(1, 3)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Improving Language Understanding by
Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2018, §3.3.

Pooling picks index $t^\star = \sum_t \mathbf{1}[\mathrm{mask}_t] - 1$ (the rightmost real token) per row.

attributes.name: dropout
attributes.description: Dropout layer applied to the pooled last-token embedding.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Final linear of shape `(hidden_size, num_labels)` producing
per-class logits.

members.name: GPTLMHeadModel

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None, past_key_values: Cache | None = None, use_cache: bool = False, cache_position: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: GPT-1 with a tied causal-language-modeling head.
members.extended:
Wraps `GPTModel` with an output linear whose weight matrix is
bound to the input `tokens_embed` table when
`config.tie_word_embeddings` is `True` — halving the parameter
cost of the softmax layer.  This is both the pre-training entry point
and the host of `lucid.models.CausalLMMixin.generate` for
free-form autoregressive sampling.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Hyperparameters.  `config.tie_word_embeddings` (default True)
controls weight tying.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import GPTConfig, GPTLMHeadModel
>>> cfg = GPTConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                 num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = GPTLMHeadModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, T=4, V=40478)
(1, 4, 40478)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Improving Language Understanding by
Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2018.

Loss when `labels` is supplied is the standard next-token-shifted
causal-LM cross-entropy



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CLM}}
    = -\frac{1}{B(T-1)} \sum_{b, t}
      \log p_\theta(y_{b, t+1} \mid x_{b, \le t}),
$$

with positions labelled `-100` excluded.

attributes.name: lm_head
attributes.description:
Output projection of shape `(hidden_size, vocab_size)` mapping
each token's hidden state to vocabulary logits.

members.name: GPTModel

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, past_key_values: Cache | None = None, use_cache: bool = False, cache_position: Tensor | None = None)

members.extended:
Implements the original generative pre-training architecture from
Radford, Narasimhan, Salimans, and Sutskever, 2018: a 12-layer stack
of decoder blocks with fused-QKV multi-head causal self-attention, a
2-layer feed-forward block, post-LayerNorm residuals, learned absolute
position embeddings, and a tanh-approximated GELU activation
(`gelu_new`).  Returns the per-token hidden states; the LM and
classifier heads sit on top of this trunk.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Hyperparameters controlling vocabulary, depth, width, head count,
and regularisation.  See `GPTConfig` for the full field
list.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import GPTConfig, GPTModel
>>> cfg = GPTConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                 num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = GPTModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B=1, T=4, H=128)
(1, 4, 128)
notes:
Reference: Radford, Narasimhan, Salimans, and Sutskever, *"Improving
Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical
Report, 2018.

Causal LM factorisation:



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{LM}}(\theta)
    = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}).
$$

attributes.name: tokens_embed
attributes.description:
Token embedding table of shape `(vocab_size, hidden_size)`.
HuggingFace name retained for state-dict portability.

attributes.name: positions_embed
attributes.description:
Learned absolute position embedding of shape
`(max_position_embeddings, hidden_size)`.

attributes.name: base_model_prefix
attributes.description:
State-dict prefix (`"transformer"`) under which the trunk is
nested in task-head variants.

members.name: GPTTokenizer
members.signature: GPTTokenizer(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

members.extended:
A thin convenience subclass of
`lucid.utils.tokenizer.ByteLevelBPETokenizer` with GPT-1's
default `add_prefix_space=False`.

parameters.name: vocab
parameters.description:
Token-string → id map.  Tokens are byte-mapped Unicode
strings (e.g. `"Ġworld"` for ` world`), NOT raw bytes.

parameters.name: merges
parameters.description: Ordered BPE merges; index = rank, lower = applied earlier.

parameters.name: add_prefix_space
parameters.description:
Prepend a space to the input so the first word also receives
the `Ġ` byte-mapped marker.  Off in GPT-1 (matches the
published vocab); on in RoBERTa.

parameters.name: normalizer
parameters.description: Default `lucid.utils.tokenizer.normalizers.NFC`.

parameters.name: special_tokens
parameters.description:
GPT-1 ships no canonical special tokens; pass your own if
the downstream model expects bos / eos.

see_also.name: <|endoftext|> as the default bos / eos / unk marker.

members.name: GPTTokenizerFast
members.signature: GPTTokenizerFast(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab: dict[str, int], merges: list[tuple[str, str]], add_prefix_space: bool = False, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

members.extended:
Bit-identical to `GPTTokenizer`.  The BPE merge loop runs
in C++ via the engine-side `BPE` binding for production
throughput.

Constructor parameters mirror `GPTTokenizer` — see that
class for the full reference.

members.name: gpt
members.signature: gpt(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPTWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-1 decoder-only Transformer trunk.
members.extended:
The original generative pre-training transformer from Radford, Narasimhan,
Salimans, and Sutskever, 2018: $L=12$ transformer blocks,
$H=768$ hidden, $A=12$ attention heads, intermediate width
3072, learned absolute position embeddings, and a 40,478-piece BPE
vocabulary trained on BookCorpus.  Roughly 117M parameters — the seed
of the modern decoder-only LLM family.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future weight registration.  No checkpoints are wired
yet, so the model is returned with random initialisation.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPTConfig` field overrides (e.g. `vocab_size=...`,
`hidden_size=...`, `num_hidden_layers=...`) forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: Decoder trunk configured with GPT-1 defaults plus any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import gpt
>>> model = gpt().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B=1, T=4, H=768)
(1, 4, 768)
notes:
Reference: Radford, Narasimhan, Salimans, and Sutskever, *"Improving
Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical
Report, 2018.

Causal language-model factorisation:



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{LM}}(\theta)
    = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}),
$$

enforced architecturally by a lower-triangular mask inside scaled
dot-product attention so position $t$ only attends to
$\{1, \dots, t\}$.

members.name: gpt_cls
members.signature: gpt_cls(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPTWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-1 model with a last-token sequence-classification head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `gpt` (L=12, H=768, A=12), augmented with a linear
classifier on the **last non-pad token's** hidden state — the canonical
decoder-style pooling strategy.  Use for GLUE-style classification when
you specifically want autoregressive features rather than bidirectional
BERT-style ones.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Encoder-weight selector.  `False` → fully random init; `True`
→ loads the pretrained `gpt` decoder trunk
(`GPTWeights.DEFAULT`) into the `.transformer` submodule;
a tag string selects a specific encoder checkpoint.  **The
classifier head is always randomly initialised** (fine-tuning
starting point — no GLUE-fine-tuned head ships).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit encoder-weights enum member; takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPTConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.  Pass `num_labels=N` to set the number of
output classes (default 2).  Overrides that change the trunk shape
are incompatible with loading pretrained encoder weights.

returns.description:
GPT-1 trunk wrapped with the last-token classifier head (encoder
pretrained when requested; head random).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import gpt_cls
>>> model = gpt_cls(num_labels=3).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, num_labels=3)
(1, 3)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Improving Language Understanding by
Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2018, §3.3.

Pooling picks index $t^\star = \sum_t \mathbf{1}[\mathrm{mask}_t] - 1$ (i.e. the rightmost real token) per row, then applies dropout +
linear:



$$
z = W_{\mathrm{cls}}\, \mathrm{Dropout}(h_{t^\star}) + b_{\mathrm{cls}}.
$$

members.name: gpt_lm
members.signature: gpt_lm(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: GPTLMWeights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: Construct a GPT-1 model with the tied causal-LM head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `gpt` (L=12, H=768, A=12, ~117M parameters), wrapped
with a tied LM head that reuses the input token-embedding matrix as the
output projection.  This is the pre-training entry point and the host
of `lucid.models.CausalLMMixin.generate` for free-form
autoregressive sampling.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Explicit weights enum member; takes precedence over `pretrained`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `GPTConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: GPT-1 trunk wrapped with the tied LM head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.gpt import gpt_lm
>>> model = gpt_lm().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, T=4, V=40478)
(1, 4, 40478)
notes:
Reference: Radford et al., *"Improving Language Understanding by
Generative Pre-Training"*, OpenAI Technical Report, 2018.

When `labels` is supplied the head computes the standard
next-token-shifted causal-LM loss



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CLM}}
    = -\frac{1}{B(T-1)} \sum_{b,t}
      \log p_\theta(y_{b, t+1} \mid x_{b, \le t}),
$$

with positions labelled `-100` excluded.

summary: GPT-1 family — Radford et al., 2018 (decoder-only transformer).
citation: Radford, Alec, et al. "Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training." OpenAI Technical Report, 2018.
theory:
GPT — *Generative Pre-Training* — is a decoder-only transformer trained
on an autoregressive (causal) language-modelling objective.  Given a
token sequence $x = (x_1, \dots, x_T)$, the network factorises
the joint distribution left-to-right and maximises



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}}(\theta)
    = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}),
$$

where $p_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \operatorname{softmax}(W_e h_t^{(L)})$ and $h_t^{(L)}$ is the
final-layer hidden state at position $t$.  Causality is enforced
by an upper-triangular mask inside scaled dot-product attention, so
position $t$ only attends to $\{1, \dots, t\}$.

The architecture is a stack of $L=12$ transformer blocks
(`hidden_size=768`, 12 heads, `intermediate_size=3072`) with
learned absolute position embeddings and the tanh-approximated GELU
activation (`gelu_new`).  Input and output token embeddings are
tied — the LM head reuses $W_e^\top$ — which halves the
parameter count of the softmax layer.  Unlike BERT, there is no
segment embedding and no masked-LM objective; downstream tasks are
handled by appending a task-specific delimiter sequence and reading
off the final hidden state.

Empirically, GPT established that **unsupervised pre-training on a
large unlabeled corpus** (BookCorpus, ~800M tokens) followed by
supervised fine-tuning transfers across a wide range of NLP tasks,
foreshadowing the scaling-law regime later formalised by Kaplan et
al. (2020).  Increasing depth, width, and corpus size yields smooth,
power-law improvements in held-out perplexity — the foundation that
GPT-2 and GPT-3 scale up.
